ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic toast entry; no specific kaya toast entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class 'toast (food product)' as closest_parent match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers toast bread generically. No specific kaya-and-butter toast composite entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024; closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for kaya and butter toast as a specific food concept.

serving.description: 2 slices of kaya and butter toast
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of kaya and butter toast at a Singaporean kopitiam consists of two thin slices of toasted white bread, each spread with kaya jam and a pat of cold butter, weighing approximately 80 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Kaya Toast — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for kaya and butter toast (F05020057). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kaya and butter toast as a composite dish. GI estimated at 70 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being white bread (GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database, white bread reference food). Kaya jam contributes additional rapidly-digestible sugars but butter's fat content moderately slows gastric emptying. Net GI estimated at 70 (high category). Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (70 × 37.84 × 0.80) / 100 = 21.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (14.67 g/100g) with low fibre (1.17 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 361 mg/100g and saturated fat at ~14.5 g/100g further contribute to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of kaya and butter toast; if consumed, choose wholemeal bread, request minimal kaya and butter, and pair with a low-sodium, low-sugar beverage rather than sweetened coffee or condensed-milk tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI ~70) combined with 14.67 g/100g sugar and 37.84 g/100g total carbohydrate causes rapid and significant post-prandial glucose elevation; dietary fibre at only 1.17 g/100g provides negligible glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat kaya and butter toast as an occasional food only; substituting wholegrain or multigrain bread, reducing kaya spread quantity, and avoiding concurrent sweetened beverages are the minimum modifications required to lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at approximately 14.5 g/100g (predominantly from butter and coconut-milk-based kaya) substantially exceeds the 10% of energy threshold for cardiovascular risk; trans fat at ~0.2 g/100g from butter further raises LDL cholesterol and lowers HDL cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid kaya and butter toast due to its high saturated and trans fat content; if consumed, replacing butter with a plant-based spread and reducing kaya quantity are the minimum steps to reduce atherogenic fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~14.5 g/100g (well above the 5 g/100g RA threshold) and trans fat ~0.2 g/100g from butter are strongly pro-inflammatory; sugar at 14.67 g/100g further promotes inflammatory mediator production.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid kaya and butter toast, particularly during flares; replacing butter with avocado spread and reducing kaya quantity are the minimum modifications to lower the combined saturated fat and sugar inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kaya and butter toast is a quintessential Singaporean breakfast dish served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and hawker centres across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Nanyang Chinese diaspora. It consists of thin slices of white bread — typically Hainanese-style square loaf — that are toasted over charcoal or in an electric toaster until golden and crisp, then spread generously with kaya jam and topped with a cold pat of salted butter. Kaya is a rich, fragrant coconut-egg jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaf, giving it a distinctive green or golden-brown colour and a sweet, custardy, lightly floral flavour. The cold butter melts slightly against the warm toast, creating a contrast of textures — crisp exterior, soft interior — and a balance of sweet, salty, and creamy flavours. Kaya toast is almost always served alongside soft-boiled eggs seasoned with soy sauce and white pepper, and a cup of kopi (local coffee with condensed milk) or teh (tea with condensed milk), forming the classic Singaporean kopitiam breakfast set. Nutritionally, kaya and butter toast is energy-dense at 423 kcal/100g, with high fat (27.35 g/100g, predominantly saturated at ~14.5 g/100g from butter and coconut milk), high carbohydrate (37.84 g/100g from refined white bread), and high sugar (14.67 g/100g from kaya). Dietary fibre is low at 1.17 g/100g and sodium is 361 mg/100g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 70 (high), making it unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is best enjoyed as an occasional treat rather than a daily staple.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kaya butter toast?
Is kaya toast suitable for diabetics?
What is kaya jam made of?
What is the GI of kaya toast?
Can I eat kaya toast if I have high blood pressure?
What is the fat content of kaya butter toast?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singaporean dishes) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from known composition of butter and coconut-milk kaya and documented as such.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for kaya and butter toast as a composite dish is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020057 as of 2026. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F05020057. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 g/100g: estimated from known trans fat content of butter (~0.3–0.5 g per 10 g serving) proportioned to the butter content of the dish; this is an approximation and not directly reported by HPB. saturated_fat_g set to ~14.5 g/100g: estimated from the known saturated fat composition of butter (~50% saturated fat) and coconut-milk-based kaya (~60% saturated fat of fat fraction), proportioned to their respective contributions in the dish; not directly reported as a separate field in the HPB source record. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (GI ~70) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for kaya and butter toast as a composite dish was found in approved sources.
